summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the contentious debate around Hungarian Notation and variable naming conventions, sharing his personal journey from traditional VB naming to .NET development. He explains how the transition to fully object-oriented languages forced him to reconsider whether type prefixes ever made sense. After presenting his own pragmatic conventions focused on simplicity and readability, he argues that internal consistency within a team matters far more than finding a perfect universal standard. He advocates for leaving room for personal interpretation rather than over-standardizing in areas without definitive right answers.
key_points: Hungarian Notation is a religious war topic with extreme polarity in developer opinions
In fully OO languages where everything is an object, type prefixes like 'obj' become meaningless
Good naming conventions prioritize simplicity, readability, and distinguishing scope over comprehensive type prefixes
Internal team consistency matters more than adhering to any particular 'perfect' naming standard
Over-standardization in areas without clear right answers is soul-crushing and counterproductive
money_quotes: What naming scheme do you use for a fully OO language where... everything is an object? objEverything isn't very satisfying.
I do not believe every single object needs a unique prefix. This is insane, and as the VB6 document illustrates, this way leads madness...
Pointing out how stupid someone's variable names are is like telling them how stupid they are for naming their first born child 'Melvin.'
It's more important to be internally consistent with a naming standard than it is to spend a lot of time sussing out some kind of perfect, interplanetary naming standard.
There's nothing quite as soul crushing as over-standardizing in a religious area where there really isn't a 'right' answer.
Naming conventions cannot guarantee good code, however; only the skill of the programmer can.
key_insight: Naming conventions should prioritize team consistency and readability over rigid adherence to any universal standard, since there is no definitively correct answer in this religious debate.
